title: Chapter 10: Noon at the Rot King's Throne
content: Noon in Greyhaven was what midnight was on the surface. Quiet. Still. Dead.

Kael walked through the empty streets with Sera at his side and Marcus's map folded in his pocket. The torches still burned in their iron sconces — no one extinguished them during the day, because no one was awake to do it — but the flames seemed dimmer somehow. Diminished. Waiting for the night to return and give them purpose.

The outer gate was open. Of course it was. The guards who should have been manning it were slumped against the walls, their eyes closed, their breathing so shallow it barely disturbed the air. Baron-rank vampires, hardened soldiers who'd served Dredd for decades, reduced to corpses waiting to wake. Kael walked past them without breaking stride.
"The inner sanctum is next," Sera murmured. "Then the throne room."
"Are you sure you're ready for this?"

"I'm your anchor. If I'm not ready, you die." Her voice was steady, but he could feel her fear through the Pact — a cold thread woven through the golden warmth of their bond. "Don't worry about me. Focus on the plan."

The inner sanctum was a spiral staircase carved from obsidian, its walls lined with niches where more guards slept. Fledglings, mostly. Young vampires who'd been turned for their strength and conditioned for their obedience. They didn't stir as Kael climbed past them. Didn't flinch. Didn't dream.
At the top of the stairs, the throne room door.

It was iron and silver, warded with blood magic that pulsed faintly in the torchlight. A lock that would have taken a human thief hours to pick. A barrier that would have stopped any vampire below Count rank.
Kael studied it for thirty seconds. Then he smashed it with his shoulder.

Baron strength. The door buckled. The wards flared and died. The lock shattered into a dozen pieces of twisted metal that clattered across the stone floor. The noise echoed through the Keep like a thunderclap — and for a moment, Kael thought he'd woken them all. Thought the guards would come flooding up the stairs, the Barons and fledglings and thralls, all of them suddenly awake and furious and very, very dangerous.
Nothing moved. The daylight torpor held. The noise wasn't enough to break it.
Kael stepped into the throne room.
Viscount Dredd sat on his throne.

He wasn't slumped like the guards. He was *sitting*, ramrod straight, his long thin fingers gripping the armrests of a chair carved from human bone. His eyes were closed, but his posture was ready — like a king who'd fallen asleep in court and expected to wake at any moment. His skin was stretched tight over a skull that looked more like architecture than anatomy. His fangs, yellowed with age, protruded slightly past his lips.
The Rot King. Two hundred years on that throne, and he looked every day of it.

Around him, the throne room was a monument to decay. Blood fountains that had long since congealed into dark crusts. Tapestries that had rotted into unrecognizable patterns. Braziers that burned incense so thick it left a film on Kael's tongue. The air smelled of old copper and older death.
"He's not what I expected," Sera whispered.
"What did you expect?"
"Something... more. A monster. A demon. Not a corpse that forgot to fall over."

Kael circled the throne, studying the ceiling. Marcus's map had marked the structural weakness on the eastern side — a jagged crack in the stone, barely visible beneath centuries of soot and neglect. He found it just where the blacksmith had said it would be, a hairline fracture that ran from the center of the ceiling all the way to the eastern wall. The patch job above it was obvious now that he was looking for it — fresh stone mortared over old damage, the work of an engineer who'd been executed before he could finish properly.
"Twelve hits," Kael murmured.
"What?"
"That's what Marcus said. Twelve solid hits to the right support beam, and the ceiling comes down."

He climbed the wall. Baron strength let him find handholds in the ancient stone — cracks and crevices that human fingers would have slipped on, ledges that human muscles couldn't have reached. He climbed until he was level with the support beam, a massive stone pillar that ran horizontally across the throne room's width.
Then he wound up and punched it.
The first hit cracked the stone.

It wasn't a subtle crack. It was a fracture that split the beam from top to bottom, spraying dust and chips of ancient mortar across the throne room. The noise was apocalyptic — a sound like a mountain waking up — and somewhere in the Keep, Kael heard the first stirrings of disturbed torpor.
The second hit widened the crack.
The third hit brought down a section of the ceiling.

Stone rained into the throne room — not the full collapse, not yet, but chunks of rock the size of human heads that crashed against the floor and shattered into shrapnel. One of them struck Dredd's throne, and Kael saw the Rot King's eyelids flutter.
He kept punching.

Five. Six. Seven. The beam was screaming now, a structural groan that resonated through the entire Keep. More guards were stirring — he could hear them below, confused barks and the scrape of claws on stone as vampires struggled to shake off their torpor before it was fully dark.

Eight. Nine. The ceiling cracked in a dozen places. Dust filled the air until Kael could barely see. Sera had pressed herself against the far wall, her hand on her sword, her eyes fixed on the throne where Dredd was definitely, unmistakably waking up.
Ten. Dredd's eyes opened.
"What—"
Eleven. The ceiling buckled. Sunlight — real sunlight, golden and blinding and absolutely lethal — began to seep through the cracks.
Twelve.
The ceiling collapsed.

The noise was indescribable. A section of the roof nearly fifteen feet across came crashing down in a cascade of stone and dust and screaming iron. The false darkness of the throne room was obliterated in an instant, replaced by a pillar of pure sunlight that poured through the new hole in the world like judgment from a god who'd just discovered vampires existed.
Viscount Dredd didn't scream. He didn't have time.

The sunlight hit him, and two hundred years of accumulated power, two centuries of blood and tribute and terror, ignited like dry tinder. His flesh didn't burn — it *evaporated*. His bones turned to ash before they could fall. His eyes boiled out of their sockets as he reached toward Kael with a hand that crumbled to dust before it could form a fist.
When it was over, there was nothing left of the Rot King. Just a pile of grey ash on a bone throne and a single dark, pulsing jewel where his heart had been.

Kael dropped from the ceiling. His hands were bleeding — the stone had cut him, and the Pact was already flooding the wounds with Sera's healing light. He knelt beside the throne and picked up the heart-blood. It was warm. Heavier than Vereck's had been. Two hundred years of stolen power, compressed into a jewel the size of a walnut.

The doors burst open. Guards — Barons, fledglings, thralls — flooded into the throne room, their weapons drawn, their faces masks of confusion and terror. They saw the collapsed ceiling. They saw the sunlight. They saw the throne covered in ash.
And they saw Kael, standing in the sunlight, unharmed.

There were thirty-seven of them. Thirty-seven vampires who'd served the Rot King for decades, who'd prospered under his corruption, who were now staring at the impossible thing that had killed him in his own throne room.

"I am Kael Ashworth," he said. His voice carried through the ruined chamber, amplified by his Baron power. "I killed Viscount Dredd. By right of conquest, his territory is mine. His throne is mine. His city is mine."
A Baron in the front row stepped forward. Older, scarred, probably Dredd's lieutenant. "You killed him in daylight. With *sunlight*. That's—"

"That's what?" Kael turned to face him. The sunlight framed his silhouette like a halo of fire. "The law says 'He who slays his own kind shall die.' But I didn't kill him. The sun killed him. I just opened the door."
The Baron had no answer. None of them did.
Kael raised the heart-blood, dark and pulsing and full of two centuries of stolen strength.
"The Rot King is dead," he said. "Long live the King."
And he ate.
